coredata.dc:title: Facile one-pot electrochemical synthesis and encapsulation of carbon quantum dots in GUVs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Guzmán-López, José E.
Pérez-Isidoro, Rosendo
Amado-Briseño, Miguel A.
Lopez, Israel
Villareal-Chiu, Juan Francisco
Sánchez-Cervantes, Eduardo M.
Vázquez-García, Rosa A.
Espinosa-Roa, Arián
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) featured with blue fluorescence were prepared by electrochemical synthesis at room temperature using carbon electrodes as a carbon source and encapsulated in liposomes of DMPC (1,2-dimyristoyl-sn‑glycero-3-phosphocholine) in a single quick step. The system liposome-CQDs was characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and epifluorescence microscopy. Electrochemical studies, such as cyclic voltammetry and impedance, have corroborated that electrosynthesis occurs at a voltage of -300 mV to form the hybrid system. The analysis includes the evaluation of variables with effect on the synthetic process of CQDs: voltage and reaction time. The results revealed that liposomes labeled with the CQDs are suitable for optical characterization by conventional and epifluorescence microscopies. In summary, this research involved the synthesis of water-soluble CQDs and their encapsulation in phospholipid vesicles in one pot, their characterization using various analytical techniques, the investigation of crucial synthesis variables, and particularly for visualization purposes in microscopy. Due to the rapidity and simplicity of the synthesis method, this work could have potential applications in analytical chemistry, biology, materials science, and related fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Liposomes
Electrosynthesis

JEGL thanks CONAHCYT for the doctoral grant 791378 . RPI and MAAB acknowledge CONAHCYT the postdoctoral grant numbers 3969865 and 709013 through Estancias Posdoctorales por México program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process for the hybrid system GUVs-CQDs.
FT-IR spectra GUVs-CQDs (blue), LUVs (red), and CQDs (black).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of GUVs-CQDs (lilac curve), CQDs (green curve), GUVs-CQDs PL spectra (red curve, λem = 341 nm), and excitation spectra (blue dashed line, λex = 215 nm).
Cyclic voltammetry of CQDs-GUVs in 5 mM KCl, 10 mV/s, under nitrogen.
Nyquist Impedance diagram to 100 kHz-0.1 Hz with different voltages. The lines in the Nyquist plot represents the fittings of the data. a) Equivalent circuit, b) V vs log Rct graph.

SEM images of GUVs-CQDs, A) agglomeration of GUVs-CQDs system, B) CQDs or graphite remnant, C) encapsulation of CQDs and D) Optical microscopy of vesicles in solution. The images were processed to enhance the derived GUVs-CQDs system.

Fluorescence properties of the GUVs-CQDs system. Horizontal images applying different filters corresponding to A) Free CQDs, B) Hybrid GUVs-CQDs system and C) unstable GUVs-CQDs system; CQDs outside the lipid.

head.ce:title: Facile one-pot electrochemical synthesis and encapsulation of carbon quantum dots in GUVs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) featured with blue fluorescence were prepared by electrochemical synthesis at room temperature using carbon electrodes as a carbon source and encapsulated in liposomes of DMPC (1,2-dimyristoyl-sn‑glycero-3-phosphocholine) in a single quick step. The system liposome-CQDs was characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and epifluorescence microscopy. Electrochemical studies, such as cyclic voltammetry and impedance, have corroborated that electrosynthesis occurs at a voltage of -300 mV to form the hybrid system. The analysis includes the evaluation of variables with effect on the synthetic process of CQDs: voltage and reaction time. The results revealed that liposomes labeled with the CQDs are suitable for optical characterization by conventional and epifluorescence microscopies. In summary, this research involved the synthesis of water-soluble CQDs and their encapsulation in phospholipid vesicles in one pot, their characterization using various analytical techniques, the investigation of crucial synthesis variables, and particularly for visualization purposes in microscopy. Due to the rapidity and simplicity of the synthesis method, this work could have potential applications in analytical chemistry, biology, materials science, and related fields.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, nanotechnology has represented a field of exhaustive investigation of the extraordinary properties of matter at the nanometer scale with enormous implications physically, chemically, and biologically [1,2]. The aim is to take advantage of the multiple properties of matter from the atomic and molecular levels, as is the case of nanoparticles (NPs). NPs have attracted significant attention due to their cost-effectiveness, benefits, and applications in different areas, such as nanopharmacology, tissue engineering, cell imaging, and biomarkers [1–3]. Furthermore, the green and sustainable synthesis of these types of NPs makes an impact by omitting and reducing the use of chemical and toxic reagents [4]. Among the wide variety of existing nanomaterials (NMs), quantum dots currently stand out due to their broad applications and the fact that this year's 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Moungi Bawendi, Louis Brus and Alexei Ekimov for the discovery and synthesis of these materials [5].

The assembly of NMs with allotropy properties [6], (a term attributed to carbon dots, CDs)[7,8] involves carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [9], graphene nanocomposites (GNs) [10], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [11,12], polymeric dots (PDs) [13] and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [3,6,7,14,15]. Although these NMs exhibit similar optical properties and surface states, they can be differentiated based on their intrinsic internal structures [14]. CQDs have become a sophisticated material due to their exceptional biocompatibility, excellent physicochemical properties, and mainly, their unique, highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property [16–18]. However, LP is a matter of debate, suggesting multiple mechanisms for its explanation; some research groups attribute it to the size distribution of the CQDs (quantum confinement effect), in addition to the different emissive traps on their surface [18,19]. Others mention that it is achieved through chemical reduction, modifying, and raising the number of sp3-type carbon defects [20]. It has been reported that CQDs possess sizes less than 10 nm [7,8,23,24], and are classified as zero-dimensional material [22] with sp [2] carbon structure. Also, it has been postulated the existence of a diamond-like structure formed by sp [3] carbons with an evident crystal lattice with typical interlayer distances of approximately 0.34 nm22 corresponding to the (002) spacing of crystalline graphite [25] and lack of band gap allowing fluorescence [14,16,18]. At the same time, CQDs presents an optical absorption in the UV–Vis region between 230 and 360 nm [14,16], including the ability to modulate the emission wavelength by modifying the particle size [16,23]. Their surface structure possesses complete carbonization and oxygen-containing functional groups [21,22,26] such as carboxyl, amide, hydroxyl, epoxide, and carbonyl residues, resulting in excellent water solubility and easy coupling with other materials [14,16,25,27,28]. CQDs properties have been postulated for multiple applications, including analytical assays, photocatalytic, physicochemical, photoluminescent, optical, optoelectronic, biodetection, bioimaging, drug delivery, light emitting diodes, and storage devices [6–8,15,17,25]. CQDs are considered the next generation of green NMs [25] due to their production from various low-cost materials, such as powdered graphite, carbon rods, carbon nanotubes, or candle soot [14].

However, for the synthesis of several of these materials, heavy metallic elements of periodic groups II-VI or III-V are used, an alarming situation due to their potential toxicity [22,29], and the use of large amounts of strong acids, high temperatures as in high-energy ion beam radiation methodologies, laser ablation on carbon-based materials [19,20], hydrothermal method [30,31], ultrasound, wet chemical method, arc discharge and microwaves [22,23,25], create problems for their application [19,23]. In response to this situation, oxidation-related routes, such as electrosynthesis, have been proposed to produce these NMs [15]. This technique is sophisticated due to the advantages of low cost, easy processing, high yield, reduced cytotoxicity, and adequate reproducibility [21,25], allowing the study, modulation, and establishment of parameters in real-time, such as the induced current [21]. Ideally, for the generation of these NMs is that they should be bright, non-toxic, biocompatible, and photobleaching-stable imaging agents [2,3], offering outstanding characteristics for applications in optical imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [32,33], making them a model of "personalized medicine", with characteristics and criteria of modern therapeutics [17], as is the case with liposomes. These submicron spherical structures were discovered in England in the early 1960s [34–38] and are used as a model of membranes to study biomolecular functions. Liposomes are self-assembled structures of lipid bilayers in aqueous media due to the amphiphilic nature of their components, mainly including phospholipids, cholesterol, sphingolipids, glycolipids, long-chain fatty acids, and even membrane proteins [39,40]. Such compositions lead to liposomes accomplishing excellent biocompatibility to rule out toxicity problems in several nanodevices for biomedical applications. During their formation process, these structures allow active encapsulation, where water-soluble materials can remain in the aqueous cavity, while hydrophobic materials can be trapped in the lipid bilayer [41].

According to the vesicle size, liposomes are mainly classified into as small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs, 20–100 nm42), large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs, >100 nm42) and giant unilamellar vesicles (GUVs, with sizes > 1 µm42). These nanodevices are excellent systems for encapsulating hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs [37]. Liposome preparation methods include lipid hydration [43], detergent depletion, and regulation of liposome sizes through extrusion techniques with fixed diameter pores [37]. Recently, the electrosynthesis technique has been incorporated as a two-step methodology based on deposition on electrodes and the instantaneous formation of lipid vesicles in aqueous media [43].

Liposomes are of great utility due to their size and amphipathic character (both hydrophobic and hydrophilic) [40,44] with technological applications in biodetection, separation of metal ions, cosmetics, imaging agents [45,46], and mainly as drug carrier systems [38,42,47–51]. The importance of the study of encapsulation in these lipid systems is focused on the protection of the drug against naturally occurring degradation phenomena, such as enzymatic processes, chemical, and immunological inactivation, protection against pH and light [37,52], but despite multiple studies, the different properties described still present instability and premature release of encapsulated materials. On the other hand, the permeable nature of liposomes hinders their potential application in nanomedicine and reproducibility on an industrial scale [44,49]. To circumvent this problem, structural and surface modifications have been made on lipid molecules in order to incorporate functionalized additives such as metal nanoparticles, amphiphilic proteins, even quantum dots, forming hybrid NMs. These can be obtained from different methodologies such as spin-coating, evaporation-induced assembly, coadsorption from mixed micelle solutions of lipids and surfactants, as well as vesicle fusion, the latter being, a simple alternative for the formation of lipid composition [53]. The term hybrid NMs is an innovative concept attributed to the interaction between liposomes and NMs such as CQDs; it is believed that hydrophobic NPs and appropriately sized CQDs can be localized within the membrane, while cationic and hydrophilic particles can interact with the surface of the bilayer [55]. These NMs present significant opportunities for the design of nanoscale theranostic delivery systems [37], applications in biotechnology [56,57], as well as cell imaging and drug delivery [53,55,58]. In addition, they have emerged as promising stabilizers, significantly in both in vitro and in vivo colloidal dispersions [41].

He Xiao et al. recently developed a simple and innovative method for obtaining NPs, such as CQDs, using electrochemical synthesis and a carbon-based material [21,26]. In this context, we present a novel and simple methodology for generating a hybrid system under normal pressure and temperature conditions through electrosynthesis. We omit expensive equipment, high temperatures, and acids and replace the latter with a biologically sustainable and relevant medium (water-electrolyte).

We generated a hybrid GUVs-CQDs system, contributing to an ecological, practical, and efficient methodology with the possibility of modulating its characteristics through operational parameters. This system shows dispersibility in water and a blue luminescence; it was thoroughly characterized by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared and cyclic voltammetry. Our results will pave the way for future biomedical applications.

1,2-Dimyristoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DMPC) and KCl (98 %) were from Sigma-Aldrich and used without prior purification. A Merck Millipore system obtained deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩcm. Graphite electrodes (BF2–3) were used as received. For more information about electrode characteristics, see Table S1 of the Electronic Supplementary Information.

Using a mini-extruder (Avanti Polar Lipids), extrusion and preparation of large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) of DMPC was done from multilamellar vesicles (MLVs) dispersed in deionized water. First, a suspension of MLVs was prepared from the hydration of 4 mg of DMPC in 2 mL of deionized water at 37 °C and homogenized at 1000 rpm for 2 h. Subsequently, the MLVs suspension underwent extrusion on a polycarbonate membrane (Nucleopore Track-Etched Membrane, Whatman) with a pore size of 100 nm for obtaining uniform in size liposomes and monodisperse LUVs; MLVs were extruded 21 times at 37 °C. The hydrodynamic diameter of the liposomes was checked by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer (Malvern, UK) equipped with a photodiode laser (830 nm) and a 90° optical arrangement, confirming the liposome size at 100 nm as observed in the DLS size distribution (Figure S1. of the Electronic Supplementary Information). Finally, the LUVs suspension was diluted to 20 µL to coat the electrodes.

The synthesis procedure for the GUVs-CQDs system from graphite is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Only two simple consecutive steps are described; once the LUVs are generated, Microdroplets (105 µL) of LUVs are deposited on the surface of graphite electrodes (≈1 cm2) at 40 °C, obtaining a thin film on the surface. The electrodes were then immersed in 5 mL of KCl electrolyte (5 mM) with a voltage of 2.5 Vs, 2 min cycles per electrode, and a reaction time of 8 min at room temperature.

Cyclic voltammetry was performed on a potentiostat/galvanostat IKA Electrochemistry ElectraSyn 2.0 pro in deoxygenated solutions of 5 mM of KCl in Milli-Q water at room temperature (25 °C) with 3-electrode cell in cathodic direction with a −2 V to 0 V potential interval at a scanning speed of 10 mVs−1. The working electrode was a carbon electrode. The compounds were deposited on the working electrode and GUVs were obtained in situ; an Ag/AgCl (KCl) electrode was used as a reference, and the counter electrode consisted of a platinum-coated copper substrate.

The synthesized GUVs-CQDs was characterized by using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GUVs-CQDs, were recorded with a SHIMADZU UV-1900i spectrophotometer using standardized 1.0 cm quartz cells. The sample synthesized were carried out in the absorption wavelength of 200 nm to 600 nm at room temperature. Fluorescence microscopy images of GUVs-CQDs was observed using a Leica microsystems DM3000 fluorescence microscope with DAPI ET filter, "k" size, with excitation: BP 350/50, dichroic mirror: LP 400, and emission BP 460/50. The surface functional groups were analyzers by using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum was measured using an FTIR Nicolet model Nexus (Thermo Nicolet, USA), using a diamond crystal accessory for attenuated total reflectance (ATR), from 4000 to 500 cm−1; with a resolution of 4 cm−1. For this end, liposome-CQDs samples were prepared over an aluminum surface depositing 50 µL of sample and dried at room temperature. The morphological image of liposome-CQDs was obtained by Field Emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EC) JEOL model JSM-7401F, which has a Noran-200 model EDX equipment coupled, the liposome-CQDs sample was prepared on glass surface by deposition 50 µL of sample and dried at room temperature. Voltammetry measurements were recorded on an ElectraSyn 2.0 device using two electrodes, carbon and platinum. Measurements of surface tension pendant drop methodology were recorded using Python software.

Spectroscopic tests were performed to understand the interaction of the GUVs-CQDs system. Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra in the dry film state with their respective vibrational spans. The GUVs-CQDs system (blue spectrum) shows five main vibrational stretches, which overlap with the spectra of the lipid system and the CQDs. It is possible to observe for the GUVs-CQDs system a broad longitudinal vibration and intense signal in the range of 3600–3080 cm−1 corresponding to the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups (OH)[3,19,24,25,29,59–61] related to the hydrophilicity and stability of CQDs in aqueous systems [15,31,61,62]. Three vibrational stretches were also observed at 2917 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, and 803 cm−1 corresponding to C-H groups [49,63], furthermore, at 1736 cm−1 the characteristic vibration corresponding to the symmetrical stretching of the carbonyl group (C=O) is observed [23,24,29,49,59–61,64–66]. Likewise, a vibrational stretch is observed and displaced at 1260 cm−1 (compared to the lipid system with a vibrational stretch at 1050 cm−1) corresponding to the negatively charged phosphate group (PO),[46,49,64–67] this signal was pointed out, and is attributed to the interaction of the CQDs-PO, taking into account that the CQDs have a positive charge and can bind in negatively charged areas,[67] in addition, the possible formation of H bonds or coulombic interactions.[64] Continuing with the GUVs-CQDs system, an intense vibrational stretch is observed, but it shifts to lower frequencies in 1010 cm−1, attributed to the interaction of the CQDs in the lipid system, it reflects a lower degree of freedom, obtaining a more rigid and stable surface in the liposomes.[64] It is observed that the vibrational stretches at 1160 cm−1, 1150 cm−1 and 970 cm−1 corresponding to C-O-C, PO 3,15,25,66 and N(CH3)3+ (amine group)[49,64,65] symmetrical of the LUVs system, are integrated in this signal. It is essential to mention that the different interaction strengths reported by various research groups constitute a lipophilic inner compartment that acts as a permeability barrier, both inward and outward [37]. Therefore, the FTIR results indicate that the surfaces of the CQDs were completely hydrophilic, probably the reason for the excellent dispersibility in water [68] as they present precise hydroxyl and carbonyl groups.

To investigate the optical properties of the LUVs, CQDs, and GUVs-CQDs systems, UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometry measurements were performed with a measurement range of 200 to 500 nm and excitation and emission-dependent fluorescence spectra. The CQDs and GUVs-CQDs systems showed a broad absorption band at 230–325 nm (Fig. 3 ), a typical characteristic of fluorescent CQDs [36,61], confirming the presence of QCDs in the hybrid system and omitting the LUVs system whose absorption bands are null for this range. The GUVs-CQDs system presents three main absorption bands: a maximum band at 246 nm related to the interaction of the CQDs with the liposomes with a clear hypsochromic and hyperchromic shift, in addition to two absorption bands, one at 295 nm corresponding to the π - π* transitions of the C=C bonds and the sp [2] domains that integrate the CQDs and the second relatively strong absorption band at 305 nm corresponding to the n - π* transition of the C=O bond, which would indicate a higher degree of oxidation of the CQDs [22,24,59–61]. The obtained aqueous suspension was achromatic and transparent to daylight, but when irradiated with a 405 nm laser, it emitted blue luminescence (Fig. 3, inset). A laser at λex = 306 nm (blue dashed line) was used to study the photoluminescence in the GUVs-CQDs system, obtained a PL emission signal with a bathochromic shift of λem = 341 nm (red line), characteristic of CQDs [69,70].

The emission wavelength of the GUVs-CQDs system showed a point and minimum redshift, attributed to the effect of homogeneous particle size and surface chemistry [14,15]. These results converge to surface oxidation in CQDs and directly impact PL [25]. Theoretically, PL behavior is related to the specific functional groups (mainly C=O)[18], the decrease of surface defects and a uniform size in the molecule [24,71] generating energy transitions in the π-conjugated domains and the sp [2] and sp [3] carbon core [14–16,27,29].

To obtain information about the reduction processes undergone by lipid membranes when subjected to the electrosynthesis process, CV studies were carried out. The CQDs and GUV-CQDs were studied by cyclic voltammetry (according to Fig. 4 ) and the voltammogram was recorded (during the sweep from the anode to the cathode). In the inset (green points) observing for the carbon electrode that it presents a more significant faradaic reaction of −1.17 mA/cm2 compared to the GUV-CDQs that they present −0.7 mA/cm2; which suggests a lower interaction of the doped vesicles with the carbon quantum dots with respect to the medium (Fig. 4). After adding the solution with liposomes, there is a decrease in the current caused by adding liposomes. Therefore, the GUVs particles contributed to a reduction of the metallic state and a notable deposition on the surface of the carbon electrode.

The contribution of the liposomes deposited on the electrodes demonstrates a higher stability due to the deceleration of the electric current in the reduction probe, which makes the electron transfer induced by the surface modification easier.

Likely, the lipid deposition on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode is stabilized through hydrophobic interactions between the DMPC hydrocarbon chains and that of the aqueous solution (KCl) molecules.

We used EIS to track the system's behavior, as shown in Fig. 5 . The black line shows the resistive behavior of the carbon electrode; however, the rest of the impedances were made with a layer of the liposome covering the electrode. Higher impedance changes indicate the presence of an insulating material on the electrode surface that affects electrode ion transfer. The addition of the lipid layer on the carbon electrode resulted in a significant change that can be observed in higher impedance due to the presence of the layer that reduces the flux of ions directly to the electrode by increasing voltage. In the Nyquist plot, shown in Fig. 5, we can see the negative values of the imaginary impedance and the actual impedance in a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. This graph allows us to obtain the electrical properties of the layer using an equivalent circuit model for the system (Fig. 5a).

The semicircular formation in the Nyquist diagram is represented by an equivalent circuit, according to Fig. 5(a); where Rs (Solution Resistance), Rct (Charge Transfer Resistance), W (Warburg Constant) and CPE (Constant Phase Element) are represented. It is configured as follows; in series with CPE//(Rct-W(series), suggests capacitive behavior of the liposome film deposited on the carbon electrode. As a point of comparison, we show the Nyquist diagram of the carbon electrode alone at −100 mV; (C-100 mV), compared to a gradual increase in microvoltages, using a CPE in parallel with an Rct-W serial array; in a system (10 % aqueous KCl), which further corroborates the sensitivity of the method to detect interactions. Finally, to further explore the effects of increasing voltage on the electrical properties of the lipid layer, they were run in 100 mV increments from −100 mV to −600 mV, with the liposome film coating the electrode represented in the graph as (L-(100–600 mV)).

At the beginning of the experiment, the behavior of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is shown, with values greater than 11 KΩ, as seen in Table 1 . However, at −300 mV, a significant decrease in Q is observed with a value of 0.24 µF/cm2. Therefore, we assume that electrosynthesis begins precisely at that point, thus forming the GUVs-CQDs hybrid system, which agrees with the cyclic voltammetry performed previously. However, as the negative voltage gradual increase continues, the charge transfer resistance shows a significant reduction of 4.1 KΩ, corresponding to −500 mV, as shown in Fig. 5b, which corresponds to the generation of hydrogen [72].

Of great importance in this study is the fact that the variation in the impedance of the CPE can be correlated with the variation in the VCs of the vesicle film formed on the carbon surface. The capacitance depends on the dielectric permittivity and the dielectric film thickness of the bilayer. Therefore, we can correlate the decrease in capacitance values with the reduction of vesicle film thickness and the decline in the dielectric permittivity of the membrane vesicle film, where the formation of a vesicle film is suggested due to membrane fluidity or liposome accumulation on the carbon surface, changes in the equilibrium between the free bilayer and CQDs buried into the phospholipid membrane.

By studying interfacial tension (IFT), we can ascertain the interfacial properties in the hybrid system and clarify their possible applications. The system was assembled as described by Berry et al., incorporating some modifications [71] as a humidity and temperature sensor inside a closed system, trying to optimize the interfacial tension values in real time (Figure S2 of the Electronic Supplementary Information). Table S2 shows the values obtained in each system, and they are compared with those reported in the literature, taking as a reference the values of water, the main solvent in which our compound was obtained [71,73]. The IFT values recorded are very similar, and the differences between these data are so minimal that they can be disregarded, as in the case of the spectroscopic and optical characterization tests. This simple but significant variation allows us to clarify that the chemical composition of each sample is not affected by the presence of the CQDs, mainly in the generation of our GUVs-CQDs hybrid material, giving a guideline for the possible use of this hybrid system in biological applications.

Fig. 6 shows typical scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. In A) an agglomerate of the obtained GUVs-CQDs material is observed, while the upper right inset, B) represent structures of the CQDs or graphite agglomerates that could not be encapsulated due to their size; these isolated agglomerations can be attributed to the instability of the liposomes, when drying the sample. On the other hand, C) represent the encapsulation and generation of GUVs-CQDs systems; as observed in solution, through optical microscopy (Fig. 6D). The formation of these hybrid systems is indicated by yellow arrows. To confirm the generation of the hybrid system, EDX analysis of the GUVs-CQDs sample indicates that 17.17 % wt of carbon is present in the sample as CQDs material [6]. (Fig. S3 of the Electronic Supplementary Information).

The synthesized GUVs-CQDs system was investigated under a fluorescent microscope to describe the possible processes that make up their encapsulation. 5 µL aliquots of GUVs-CQDs were taken and deposited at room temperature on an object holder, covered by a coverslip, and observed under the fluorescent microscope with a DAPI ET filter. Fig. 7 shows three possible processes that comprise the CQDs encapsulation, which can be observed in their blue photoluminescence. However, the images presented exemplify how these systems can be found from their intrinsic formation, recorded under standard ambient conditions (SATP). We hereby describe each image section from its photoluminescence: Section A shows the free CQDs material with bright blue and colored photoluminescence [62]. Subsequently, section B, by the circular outline, can be attributed to the accommodation of the CQDs inside the vesicle since the lipid does not generate photoluminescence as described in the UV–Vis spectra. In contrast, in section C, the deformation of the vesicle is observed, releasing the CQDs material because the solid electrostatic attractions would cause the deformation of the membrane and its morphological alteration [36,53–55,58,67,68,71,73–75]. Although it is known that carbon NPs affect the stability of a lipid bilayer due to their hydrophobic nature [76], the system we found represents a significantly novel system in the stabilization and formation of a hybrid system, reported for the first time.

We obtained and confirmed the GUVs-CQDs hybrid system through different spectral and optical characterization techniques. We contribute to the conceptualization that liposomes, being administration systems, can also be considered as carriers of different types of nanoparticles [77], taking into account properties such as size and lamellarity, in addition, post-formation processes such as sonification or extrusion [37], are determining factors for optimal encapsulation and greater versatility in obtaining this type of hybrid materials. Also, we established a simple method for the generation of GUVs-CQDs hybrid systems from the electrosynthesis methodology, highlighting three fundamental characteristics: (1) we consider our methodology as green chemistry, using water as the reaction medium, which makes it completely sustainable and environmentally friendly, in addition, we established a "record" reaction time (8 min) compared to other techniques that require multiple steps, large volumes of acids, and high temperatures; (2) the synthesis method uses commercial graphite sources, economizing the reaction and (3) it does not require sophisticated equipment, generating the materials under normal conditions of pressure and temperature.

The NMs obtained maintain good fluorescence optical properties and stability in water; these unprecedented results provide valuable and innovative structural information about the nature of nanoparticle-lipid interactions, and from their electrochemical synthesis, offer important insights into the rapid, facile and sustainable production of hybrid NMs. These factors are of utmost importance that could have implications for future applications in optical biodetection, drug delivery and cytotoxicity. It will be useful and interesting to further examine in detail the structural chemical evolution for the optimization of these nanomaterials from size control to their modulation in the different fluorescence ranges.

Despite the numerous investigations about these hybrid systems, there is an infinity of undetermined knowledge about the NPs-membrane interaction, but we try to delimit, and establish parameters that help various research groups to modulate specific sizes regarding nanoparticles, degree of hydrophilicity, surface charge, functionality, etc. In addition, electrochemical studies allowed us to corroborate that electrosynthesis is present at a voltage of −300 mV for the formation of the hybrid system. This multidisciplinary study expands opportunities in the field of scientific research for future applications and generation of new materials.

José E. Guzmán-López: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Rosendo Pérez-Isidoro: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Miguel A. Amado-Briseño: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Israel Lopez: Investigation, Data curation. Juan Francisco Villareal-Chiu: Investigation, Data curation. Eduardo M. Sánchez-Cervantes: Investigation, Data curation. Rosa A. Vázquez-García: Supervision, Data curation. Arián Espinosa-Roa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Arian Espinosa Roa reports financial support was provided by Consejo Nacional de Humanidades, Ciencias y Tecnologías. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by CONAHCYT-Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación 2017–2018/A1-S-13267. JEGL thanks CONAHCYT for the doctoral grant 791378. RPI and MAAB acknowledge CONAHCYT the postdoctoral grant numbers 3969865 and 709013 through Estancias Posdoctorales por México program.
